prompt:
Passage 1
A regional warehouse recently updated its process for handling damaged incoming shipments. Previously, receiving employees set damaged boxes aside and wrote a note for a supervisor. During busy periods, some notes were delayed or lacked enough detail for the supervisor to decide what action was needed.
The new process requires employees to enter damage reports into a shared tracking system. Each report includes the supplier name, shipment number, type of damage, photos if available, and whether any items appear unusable. Supervisors review the reports throughout the day and decide whether to accept the shipment, request replacement items, or contact the supplier.
Managers report that the new process has improved communication between receiving staff and supervisors. It has also created clearer records when the company needs to follow up with suppliers.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The warehouse no longer receives shipments.
choices.B: A shared tracking system improved how damaged shipments are handled.
choices.C: Suppliers now inspect all warehouse shelves.

explanation: The passage focuses on how a shared tracking system improved the reporting and handling of damaged incoming shipments.

prompt:
Passage 2
NOTICE
Beginning October 14, all employees using the shared battery charging station must write the battery number and start time on the charging log. Batteries should be removed promptly once charging is complete so the station remains available for other users.
The updated procedure was created after several employees reported that fully charged batteries were left connected for long periods. This caused delays for workers who needed to charge additional batteries before beginning assigned tasks.
Supervisors will check the charging log at the end of each shift.

What is the purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To announce a new procedure for using the battery charging station
choices.B: To close the charging station
choices.C: To explain how to purchase new batteries at home

explanation: The notice announces the updated procedure for using the shared battery charging station.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many companies use work tickets to organize service tasks. A work ticket may include the customer name, location, problem description, priority level, assigned technician, and notes about completed work.
Work tickets help employees track what needs to be done and what has already been completed. They also create a record that can be reviewed later if questions arise. For example, if a customer calls to ask why a repair took longer than expected, the ticket may show that a part had to be ordered before the job could continue.
Work tickets are most helpful when information is entered clearly. A vague description such as “unit broken” may not help the technician prepare. A more useful description explains what the customer observed and when the problem began.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Work tickets help organize service tasks when they contain clear information.
choices.B: Work tickets should include as little detail as possible.
choices.C: Customers should not be allowed to ask about repairs.

explanation: The passage explains that work tickets help organize service tasks, especially when the information is clear and useful.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before paved sidewalks were common in many towns, pedestrians often walked along dirt paths or the edges of roads. During wet weather, paths could become muddy, uneven, and difficult to use. Pedestrians also had fewer clear spaces separated from wagon or automobile traffic.
Paved sidewalks improved everyday movement by giving people a more reliable surface for walking. They helped connect homes, schools, stores, and public buildings. Sidewalks also made streets safer by creating a designated area for pedestrians.
Today, communities continue to maintain sidewalks by repairing cracks, improving curb ramps, and clearing obstacles. These efforts help sidewalks remain useful for people of different ages and abilities.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Paved sidewalks improved pedestrian movement and continue to require maintenance.
choices.B: Dirt paths are safer than paved sidewalks.
choices.C: Sidewalks are used only during wet weather.
choices.D: Communities no longer repair public walkways.

explanation: The passage explains how paved sidewalks improved walking conditions and how communities still maintain them.

prompt:
Passage 5
A regional manufacturing company recently reviewed the way it trains employees to read product specifications. Product specifications describe requirements such as size, material, finish, packaging, and acceptable tolerances. Employees use these documents to confirm that products are made and packed correctly.
The review found that some employees understood the general purpose of specifications but missed small details, such as packaging notes or finish requirements. In response, trainers added short exercises that ask employees to locate specific details in sample specification sheets.
Supervisors believe the exercises will reduce mistakes caused by overlooked information. They also plan to include more practice with comparing two similar specifications, since small differences can affect production decisions.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Product specifications are no longer used in manufacturing.
choices.B: Training exercises were added to help employees read product specifications more accurately.
choices.C: Employees now write all product specifications themselves.
choices.D: Packaging notes are unrelated to production work.

explanation: The passage explains that training was revised to help employees read specifications more accurately and notice small details.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many organizations use scheduling software to manage meeting rooms, training spaces, and shared equipment. These systems allow employees to reserve resources and check availability before making plans.
Scheduling software can reduce conflicts when many employees need access to the same resource. Instead of relying on informal conversations or paper calendars, employees can see whether a room or piece of equipment is already reserved. Some systems also send reminders before a reservation begins.
However, software works only if users keep information current. If employees reserve a room but do not cancel when plans change, others may think the room is unavailable. Clear rules for making and canceling reservations help the system remain accurate.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Scheduling software can reduce conflicts, but users must keep reservations current.
choices.B: Meeting rooms should never be reserved.
choices.C: Paper calendars are always more accurate than software.
choices.D: Employees should make plans without checking availability.

explanation: The passage explains that scheduling software reduces conflicts but depends on users keeping reservations accurate.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many communities operate public composting programs to reduce the amount of organic waste sent to landfills. Residents may be allowed to drop off food scraps, leaves, grass clippings, and other approved materials at designated collection sites.
Composting turns organic materials into a soil-like product that can be used in landscaping, gardening, or erosion control. The process works best when accepted materials are separated from trash, plastic, glass, and other contaminants.
Public instructions are important because not all organic items are accepted in every program. Clear signs and guides help residents understand what to include, what to avoid, and how to prepare materials for drop-off.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: Public composting programs help reduce organic waste and require proper sorting.
choices.B: Composting programs accept all types of trash.
choices.C: Landfills are used only for garden soil.
choices.D: Residents should mix plastic with food scraps.

explanation: The passage focuses on composting programs, accepted organic waste, and the need for proper sorting.

prompt:
Passage 8
An apprenticeship preparation course recently added timed reading drills to help students practice pacing. Instructors noticed that some students answered accurately when given unlimited time but left several questions blank during timed practice exams.
The timed drills use short workplace-style passages followed by several questions. Students practice deciding when to reread carefully and when to move on. Instructors remind students that pacing does not mean rushing; it means using time wisely so that easier questions are not missed at the end.
After several weeks, many students reported feeling less anxious about the reading section. Instructors plan to compare future practice-test results to see whether the drills improve completion rates.

Why were timed reading drills added?

choices.A: Some students left questions blank during timed practice exams.
choices.B: Students no longer needed reading practice.
choices.C: Instructors wanted students to avoid workplace-style passages.
choices.D: Practice exams were removed from the course.

explanation: Timed reading drills were added because some students left questions blank during timed practice exams.

Passage 8
An apprenticeship preparation course recently added timed reading drills to help students practice pacing. Instructors noticed that some students answered accurately when given unlimited time but left several questions blank during timed practice exams.
The timed drills use short workplace-style passages followed by several questions. Students practice deciding when to reread carefully and when to move on. Instructors remind students that pacing does not mean rushing; it means using time wisely so that easier questions are not missed at the end.
After several weeks, many students reported feeling less anxious about the reading section. Instructors plan to compare future practice-test results to see whether the drills improve completion rates.

Which statement is best supported by the passage?

choices.A: Timed practice may help students manage anxiety and improve completion.
choices.B: Students should spend unlimited time on every question.
choices.C: Easier questions should be left blank at the end.
choices.D: Timed reading drills prevent students from learning pacing.

explanation: Students reported less anxiety, and instructors plan to check whether the drills improve completion rates.
